verdict_reasoning: HMGB1 is canonically a nuclear DNA-binding protein (non-histone chromatin component), but has well-documented, extensively published active secretion to the extracellular space during inflammation, necrosis, apoptosis, and immune activation. Crucially, it also accumulates on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane during immunogenic cell death and on activated immune cells, where it can be detected by surface biotinylation and flow cytometry on intact cells. It functions as an alarmin/DAMP and signals through surface receptors (RAGE, TLR2, TLR4) — but HMGB1 itself also transiently associates with the cell surface during secretion/release. Numerous studies document its active secretion by macrophages under LPS/inflammatory stimulation and its cell-death-induced surface exposure. This constitutes a documented cell-state-induced outer-leaflet pool. The protein lacks a TM domain or GPI anchor; baseline localization is nuclear. Surface presence is therefore contextual (cell-state-induced: inflammation, ICD, activation).
